pattern_summary: All Tier 1 experiments use 10 epochs, 100-sample subsets, and a single random seed. Two of four blocks (ResNet-18 CIFAR-10: 10.01-10.97% accuracy, ViT-S/4 CIFAR-100: 2.64-2.89%) are at or within 1 pp of the random-chance floor (10% and ~1% respectively). Accuracy comparisons in these degenerate blocks cannot be attributed to augmentation ordering effects and must not be counted toward H1 confirmation. After excluding these blocks, only 2 of the remaining 2 interpretable blocks show spread > 0.5%, not '3/4' as claimed.

The paper presents ordering results (10 epochs, 100 samples) and baseline results (30 epochs, full dataset) in the same table (Table 1), making ordering-vs-baseline comparison invalid. The ordering experiments show 10-46% accuracy while the baselines show 57-92%, but this difference is entirely due to training conditions, not the methods. A reviewer will immediately flag this as methodologically unsound.

The proof sketch of the Ordering-Dependent Generalization Bound (Theorem 1) is missing the core mathematical step. The statement 'By the Lipschitz property, W_2(mu_sigma, mu_sigma') <= sum NC_2(t_i, t_j; mu)' requires a bubble-sort decomposition argument: decomposing sigma -> sigma' into a sequence of adjacent transpositions and applying the triangle inequality at each step. Without this derivation, the bound is unverified. A NeurIPS reviewer with optimal transport background will immediately request this.

The DPI Reversibility Principle has a logical gap: the contraction coefficient eta_i is defined as a worst-case quantity (supremum over all distributions p, q), making it a property of the channel alone, independent of position in the pipeline. The claim that ordering affects information loss requires that contraction be input-distribution-dependent. The worst-case definition defeats the ordering-dependence argument.

The NC_2 proxy was computed on 100 samples with 100 random projections, while the pre-registered plan specifies 10k samples and 1000 projections. The SWD estimate with n=100 has high variance for 32x32x3 = 3072-dimensional images. The current NC_2 values (Crop-CJ: 0.0515, Crop-Flip: 0.0448, Flip-CJ: 0.0348) may not be reliable estimates, and H3's falsification verdict should be marked 'preliminary pending full-scale recomputation'.

The Tier 2 category-ordering result (9.01 pp spread, interleaved P->G vs all-geometric-first) is based on a single seed, 5k samples, 10 epochs, and a single architecture-dataset combination (ResNet-18/CIFAR-10). This is the paper's most striking empirical number but has the least statistical support. The paper calls this 'the largest signal in our study' without adequately flagging that it is also the most likely to be an early-training artifact.

The Tier 3 magnitude sweep uses only 3 data points (M=5, M=9, M=14) with a single seed each. The M=14 result shows exactly identical accuracy (0.245) for both orderings to 3 decimal places, which is suspicious: either both models happened to achieve precisely the same final accuracy (unlikely with different augmentation pipelines even if they converge to similar distributions), or there is a numerical coincidence or data artifact. This needs investigation before H5 is labeled 'falsified'.

The paper's theoretical framing positions the NC bound and DPI reversibility principle as positive contributions, but both are empirically falsified in the pilot data (NC: rho=-0.20, DPI: mixed signals, overall rho=-0.06). If the full-scale experiments confirm the falsifications, the theoretical section must be reframed as 'we proposed these frameworks, tested them, and showed they fail to predict ordering quality -- here is why.' Without this reframing, the theory and evidence are in tension.

H2 is defined inconsistently across the paper. The proposal defines H2 as 'Architecture-dependent ordering sensitivity (ViTs more sensitive to geometric ordering, CNNs to photometric)'. The method section states 'ViTs and CNNs exhibit different ordering preferences'. The results section header says 'H2: Architecture-Dependent Ordering Sensitivity' but the verdict table labels it 'H2: Reversibility-sorted wins'. The DPI-based prediction (CJ->Flip->Crop outperforms conventional) is a separate testable claim that was silently substituted for the original architecture-differential hypothesis.

The InfoNCE MI estimation in Tier 4b uses 100 samples with encoders trained for only 10 epochs. At this scale, the InfoNCE estimator has high variance and may not reflect true mutual information. The CIFAR-10 rho=+0.54 (p=0.20) and CIFAR-100 rho=-0.66 (p=0.08) are both non-significant (n=6 orderings). Reporting the combined rho=-0.057 obscures the opposite signs, which is the most informative feature of this result.
